quiz_questions.question_text: What is a control group in an experiment?

options.text: The group that receives the experimental treatment being tested
The group that does not receive the treatment and is used for comparison
The group that has the most participants in the entire experiment
The group where all variables are changed at the same time for testing

quiz_questions.explanation: A control group does not receive the experimental treatment. It serves as a baseline for comparison, allowing scientists to see if the independent variable actually had an effect on the experimental group.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an experimental group?

options.text: The group where the independent variable is applied or changed
The group that is kept unchanged for comparison with others
The group of scientists who designed the experimental setup
The group that has the fewest number of participants overall

quiz_questions.explanation: The experimental group is the group that receives the treatment or where the independent variable is applied. By comparing results from the experimental group to the control group, scientists can determine the effect of the variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is a control group important in an experiment?

options.text: It eliminates the need for multiple trials during the experiment
It provides a standard for comparison to see if the treatment had an effect
It allows the scientist to change all the variables at the same time
It guarantees the hypothesis will be supported by the data results

quiz_questions.explanation: A control group is important because it provides a baseline to compare against. Without it, you cannot know whether the independent variable truly caused the observed changes or if something else was responsible.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are constants in an experiment?

options.text: Variables that are kept the same throughout all groups and trials
Variables that change randomly without any scientist controlling them
Variables that the scientist measures at the end of each trial run
Variables that are only measured in the control group of the test

quiz_questions.explanation: Constants (controlled variables) are factors that stay the same across all groups and trials. Keeping them the same ensures that only the independent variable affects the dependent variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tests whether a new fertilizer helps plants grow taller. What would be the control group?

options.text: Plants that receive the new fertilizer being tested
Plants that receive no fertilizer at all for comparison
Plants that are grown outdoors instead of in a lab
Plants that are given extra water and sunlight daily

quiz_questions.explanation: The control group would be plants that receive no fertilizer. By comparing their growth to the plants that received fertilizer, the student can determine if the fertilizer actually made a difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do scientists repeat trials in an experiment?

options.text: To increase the reliability and accuracy of their results
To make the experiment take longer than originally planned
To change the independent variable between each trial run
To prove that their hypothesis is always correct each time

quiz_questions.explanation: Repeating trials increases the reliability of results. A single trial might give unusual results due to random error. Multiple trials allow scientists to calculate averages and identify outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens if you do not control variables in an experiment?

options.text: The experiment becomes more reliable and easier to analyze
You cannot determine which variable caused the observed results
The dependent variable will not change during the experiment
The independent variable becomes easier to identify and test

quiz_questions.explanation: Without controlling variables, you cannot tell which change caused the results. Uncontrolled variables become confounding factors that make the data unreliable and the conclusions invalid.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a drug trial, some patients receive a sugar pill instead of the real medicine. What is the sugar pill called?

options.text: A supplement
A placebo
A variable
A catalyst

quiz_questions.explanation: A placebo is an inactive treatment (like a sugar pill) given to the control group. It looks like the real treatment so that patients do not know which group they are in, preventing bias from affecting results.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many trials should you run at minimum for an experiment to be considered reliable?

options.text: Only one trial is sufficient
At least three trials or more
Exactly two trials each time
It does not matter at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Scientists generally recommend at least three trials to ensure reliability. More trials provide better averages and help identify outliers. A single trial may give misleading results due to random variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the purpose of keeping conditions identical between the experimental and control groups?

options.text: To make sure any observed difference is caused only by the independent variable
To ensure the experiment produces the exact result the scientist wants
To make the experiment simpler by reducing the amount of data collected
To guarantee that both groups produce the same results every single time

quiz_questions.explanation: Keeping conditions identical between groups (except for the independent variable) ensures that any difference in results is caused only by the treatment being tested, not by some other factor.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a placebo?

options.text: An inactive treatment given to the control group that resembles the real treatment
The actual medicine or treatment given to the experimental group for testing
A measuring instrument used to record the dependent variable in experiments
A type of graph used to display the results from a controlled experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: A placebo is a fake treatment, such as a sugar pill, that looks identical to the real treatment. It is given to the control group so researchers can compare the effects of the real treatment against no treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'controlled experiment' mean?

options.text: An experiment where only the independent variable is changed while others stay constant
An experiment where the scientist already knows what the final results will be
An experiment that uses only a control group and no experimental group at all
An experiment where all variables are changed at the same time for testing

quiz_questions.explanation: A controlled experiment is one in which only the independent variable is changed while all other factors are kept constant. This design allows the scientist to establish a cause-and-effect relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bias' mean in an experiment?

options.text: A preference or influence that unfairly affects the results of an experiment
A careful and precise measurement taken during a controlled scientific test
The process of repeating an experiment to check if the results are reliable
The difference between the experimental group and the control group results

quiz_questions.explanation: Bias is any factor that systematically skews results in one direction. It can come from the experimenter's expectations, the participants' behavior, or flawed equipment. Good experiment design minimizes bias.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'trial' in an experiment?

options.text: One complete repetition of an experiment under the same conditions
The initial setup phase before any data is collected from testing
The written report produced after the experiment is fully complete
The process of changing the independent variable during the test

quiz_questions.explanation: A trial is one complete run of an experiment. Scientists conduct multiple trials under the same conditions to check if results are consistent and to reduce the impact of random errors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sample size' mean in an experiment?

options.text: The physical size of the objects being measured during the experiment
The number of subjects or items tested in an experiment overall
The length of time the experiment runs from start to finish
The amount of the independent variable applied during testing

quiz_questions.explanation: Sample size is the number of subjects, specimens, or data points included in an experiment. A larger sample size generally produces more reliable results because it reduces the effect of individual variation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'reproducibility' mean in science?

options.text: The ability for other scientists to repeat an experiment and get similar results
The ability of a scientist to predict the results before doing the experiment
The process of publishing results in a scientific journal for peer review
The requirement that every experiment must use at least 100 test subjects

quiz_questions.explanation: Reproducibility means other scientists can follow the same procedure, repeat the experiment, and obtain similar results. This is a key principle of the scientific method that builds confidence in findings.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'validity' mean for an experiment?

options.text: The experiment actually tests what it claims to test with proper controls
The experiment uses expensive, high-quality equipment for every test
The experiment produces the exact results the scientist originally predicted
The experiment is completed within the time originally set by the scientist

quiz_questions.explanation: An experiment has validity when it truly tests the hypothesis it was designed to test. This requires proper controls, a single independent variable, and elimination of confounding variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'double-blind' experiment?

options.text: An experiment where neither the subjects nor the researchers know who receives the treatment
An experiment performed in a completely dark room with no light sources available
An experiment that is repeated exactly two times to check the results for accuracy
An experiment that tests two different independent variables at the same time in trials

quiz_questions.explanation: In a double-blind experiment, neither the participants nor the researchers administering the treatment know who is in the control vs. experimental group. This prevents bias from affecting results on both sides.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'reliability' mean in a scientific experiment?

options.text: The experiment produces consistent results when repeated under the same conditions
The experiment is conducted by a well-known scientist at a major university
The experiment uses the most advanced technology available in the laboratory
The experiment produces unexpected results that surprise the scientific community

quiz_questions.explanation: Reliability means the experiment gives consistent, repeatable results. If you get very different results each time you repeat the experiment under the same conditions, the experiment is not reliable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'baseline' in an experiment?

options.text: The starting measurement or condition before any treatment is applied
The final measurement taken after the experiment has been completed
The horizontal line drawn at the bottom of every graph in science
The average of all measurements collected during the entire experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: A baseline is the initial measurement or normal condition before any experimental treatment is applied. It provides a reference point for determining whether the independent variable caused any change.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist wants to test whether a new cleaning product kills bacteria better than water alone. She applies the cleaner to one set of dishes and uses only water on another set. Which set is the control group?

options.text: The dishes cleaned with the new cleaning product
The dishes cleaned with only water and no product
Both sets of dishes are considered the control group
Neither set qualifies as the control group here

quiz_questions.explanation: The dishes cleaned with only water are the control group because they do not receive the experimental treatment (the new cleaning product). They provide the baseline for comparison.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tests whether listening to music improves memory. He has Group A study with music and Group B study in silence. Both groups take the same test. Group A scores higher. Can he conclude music helps memory?

options.text: Yes, because Group A scored higher with music playing during the study session
Not yet — he should repeat the experiment to see if the results are consistent
Yes, because one trial is always enough to confirm a scientific hypothesis works
No, because music can never influence cognitive abilities such as memory recall

quiz_questions.explanation: One trial is not enough to draw reliable conclusions. The result could be due to chance or other factors. He needs to repeat the experiment multiple times and with more participants before drawing conclusions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biologist tests a new plant food on tomato plants. She has 5 plants with the food and 5 without. After 4 weeks, the food-group plants are taller. Her colleague says the sample size is too small. Why might the colleague be right?

options.text: Five plants per group may not be enough to account for natural variation between plants
The experiment should have used a different type of plant instead of tomato plants
Four weeks is too long; the experiment should have ended after just one week
Sample size does not matter in experiments as long as a control group is included

quiz_questions.explanation: With only 5 plants per group, natural variation between individual plants could skew results. A larger sample size would give more reliable data by averaging out individual differences.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tests three paper airplane designs to see which flies the farthest. She throws each design once from the same spot. What is the biggest flaw in her experiment?

options.text: She should have thrown them from different heights to see if that matters
She should have used metal airplanes instead of paper ones for accuracy
She only tested each design once, so the results may not be reliable
She should have tested only one airplane design to keep it controlled

quiz_questions.explanation: Testing each design only once does not give reliable data. Wind, throwing force, and angle can vary between throws. She should throw each design at least three times and average the distances.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company tests a new headache medicine. Half the patients get the real pill and half get a sugar pill that looks identical. Neither the patients nor the doctors know who got which pill. What type of experiment is this?

options.text: A single-blind experiment with only one hidden variable
A double-blind experiment preventing bias from both sides
An uncontrolled experiment with no proper control group used
An observational study with no experimental treatment given

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a double-blind experiment because neither the patients nor the doctors know who receives the real medicine vs. the placebo. This prevents both groups from being influenced by expectations.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student grows bean plants under three conditions: no light, dim light, and bright light. She uses the same soil, pots, water amount, and room temperature. What are the soil, pots, water, and temperature in this experiment?

options.text: They are the independent variables being tested in this experiment
They are the constants (controlled variables) kept the same throughout
They are the dependent variables measured at the end of the test
They are experimental errors that should have been eliminated first

quiz_questions.explanation: The soil, pots, water amount, and temperature are constants (controlled variables). Keeping them the same ensures that any differences in plant growth are due to the light level (independent variable), not other factors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher asks students to test whether salt water freezes at a different temperature than fresh water. A student puts salt water in a large container and fresh water in a small container. What is the problem?

options.text: Salt water should not be used in a school laboratory experiment setting
Container size is not controlled, which could affect the freezing results
The student should have added salt to both containers for accurate results
Nothing is wrong because container size has no effect on any experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: Using different container sizes means the volume of liquid differs, which affects freezing time. Container size is a variable that should be controlled (kept the same) for a fair comparison.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist runs an experiment 10 times and gets the same result 9 times. The 10th time shows a very different result. What should the scientist do?

options.text: Throw out all the data and start the entire experiment over from scratch
Change the hypothesis to match the unusual result from the 10th trial
Identify it as a possible outlier and investigate what may have caused it
Report only the 10th trial because it is the most recent data collected

quiz_questions.explanation: The unusual result may be an outlier caused by an error or unusual condition. The scientist should investigate, run additional trials, and determine if the outlier should be included or excluded from the data analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A class experiment tests whether hand sanitizer reduces bacteria on hands. Half the students use sanitizer and half do not. Both groups press their fingers onto agar plates. A student in the sanitizer group touches the doorknob before pressing the plate. How does this affect the experiment?

options.text: It has no effect because the sanitizer already eliminated all the bacteria present
It introduces contamination that could make the sanitizer appear less effective
It improves the experiment by adding more realistic conditions to the test
It only affects the control group results and not the experimental group data

quiz_questions.explanation: Touching the doorknob after sanitizing could re-contaminate the student's hands with bacteria. This uncontrolled variable would make it seem like the sanitizer was less effective than it actually is.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tests whether the color of a cup affects how quickly water cools. She puts hot water in a white cup, a black cup, and a red cup. She places the white cup near a window and the others on a table. Why is this a flawed setup?

options.text: The student should have used cold water instead of hot water for the test
The location is not controlled; the window may cause different cooling rates
Three cups are too many; she should have only tested one cup for accuracy
Cup color cannot possibly influence the cooling rate of water inside cups

quiz_questions.explanation: Placing the white cup near a window introduces an uncontrolled variable — the window may let in cold air or sunlight that affects cooling differently. All cups should be placed in the same location to make the test fair.
